title: Wave packets from the spectrum
authors: ChunJun Cao, Oliver Friedrich, Marin Girard, Nicolas Loizeau, Ashmeet Singh
abstract: The freedom to change Fock basis seems to ensure a minimum amount of locality in lattice theories in the following sense: If $\lbrace (\hat a_i^\dagger\,,\,\hat a_i)\rbrace$ for $i=1,\dots,n$ is a lattice of creation and annihilation operators and if a given Hamiltonian $\hat H$ induces highly non-local dynamics on that lattice, then it will usually be possible to change to a new set of operators $\lbrace (\hat b_i^\dagger\,,\,\hat b_i)\rbrace$ in terms of which the dynamics appear less non-local. We demonstrate this by turning a highly non-local random matrix model into a local, 1D lattice theory where particles can propagate in localized wave packets. More generally, we show that any Hamiltonian can be made to look like such a theory, with the lattice dispersion relation and the non-integrability of the theory depending on the spectrum of $\hat H$. We argue that our results are a step towards quantum mereology for fields.
summary: The paper introduces 'quantum mereology' to decompose complex Hamiltonians into weakly interacting factors that resemble localized lattice theories. By minimizing interaction terms across these factorizations, the authors successfully map dynamics from random matrix models onto propagating wave packets. This provides a systematic theoretical pathway for extracting emergent, quasi-integrable degrees-of-freedom from highly complicated quantum systems.
main_problem: The core problem is developing a formal framework, termed 'quantum mereology,' to decompose an arbitrary quantum Hamiltonian into weakly interacting factors that mimic local lattice dynamics.
main_result: The authors show that by minimizing interaction terms in factorizations, one can successfully re-interpret the dynamics of highly non-local random matrix models (like GUE) as wave packet propagation on an emergent 1D lattice structure.
method: The methodology involves iterative Hamiltonian factorization ($\hat{H} \simeq \hat{H}_q \otimes \hat{H}_r$) by minimizing a loss function related to the interaction term, followed by mapping the resulting quasi-integrable model onto fermionic operators via Jordan-Wigner transformation.
model_or_system: The study applies to general quantum systems described by Hamiltonians ($\hat{H}$), including random matrix models (GUE) and continuum field theories like the Pauli field Hamiltonian. The focus is on finding emergent, localized degrees-of-freedom (DOFs).
key_observables: Wave packet evolution $|\langle i|\psiangle|^2$, effective dispersion relation $E_{	ext{eff}}(k)$, and the minimization of the interaction strength $	ext{Tr}(\hat{H}_{	ext{int}}^2)$.
important_parameters: The dimension $d$ of the Hilbert space, the coupling constants ($h_\ell$), and the excitation energy ($	ext{Eq}$) derived from spectral analysis.
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include approximating mutual information by $ rac{1}{2d}	ext{Tr}(\hat{H}_{	ext{int}}^2)$ and assuming that the optimal factorization can be found via local minimization of this loss function, which may fail for overlapping DOFs.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate wave packet evolution comparing non-local vs. localized dynamics; they also show spectral density plots confirming minimum interaction points (Bessel peaks) and MDS embeddings suggesting emergent 1D circular geometry.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from demonstrating the transformation of random matrices to local theories, developing the iterative factorization algorithm based on minimizing interaction loss, deriving theoretical constraints using asymptotic eigenvalue densities (GUE/Wishart), and finally applying this framework to known integrable models like the Pauli field.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful mathematical tool for understanding how complex, non-local quantum dynamics can be systematically reduced or reinterpreted as simple, local physics governed by emergent lattice modes, which is highly relevant for characterizing many-body systems in condensed matter theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Demonstrating our procedure for (re-)interpreting a given Hamiltonian ˆH as a lattice theory with local interaction structure. Left panel: evolution of an initially Gaussian wave packet wrt. random lattice dynamics, i.e. when choosing the coefficients hℓin Equation 3 at random. Right panel: switching from the initial set of lattice operators ˆa† i , ˆai to a new set ˆb† i , ˆbi via the algorithm developed in this paper. Wrt. the new lattice, states can stay localized for some time & propagate according to an effective dispersion relation. We considered a Hilbert space of dimension 211 (corresponding to 11 qubits) for this figure - see Sections 1 and 2 for details.

Figure 2: Visualizing our iterative procedure for factorizing a given Hilbert space into minimum-interaction qubits. At each iteration we consider the Hamiltonian ˆHrest,i that was left from the previous factorization as the new total Hamiltonian. We then sift through different norms | ˆHq,i+1| ∼Eq,i+1 of the qubit self-Hamiltonian and find candidates for the rest-Hamiltonian ˆHrest,i+1 such that interaction between next qubit and next rest is minimized. Among all of these candidates we then pick the qubit and rest that achieve the lowest ratio | ˆHint,i+1| / | ˆHq,i+1| . These selected factorizations are indicated by the red crosses in the figure. The upper panel displays this procedure...

Figure 3: The dispersion relations we obtain when iteratively decomposing either the Pauli field Hamiltonian or a GUE Hamiltonian into a lattice of weakly interacting spins ("Fourier modes"). See main text for details.

Figure 4: Starting from a Gaussian random Hamiltonian in a d = 211 dimensional Hilbert space we identify field operators ˆa† x wrt. which stable, propagating wave packets can be defined. Different panels show how the density |ψ(x, t)|2 evolves for wave packets of different average momenta k. Red solid lines indicate expected propagation speed from an effective dispersion relation, while black dashed lines indicate the actual center of the wave packets. Note that the packet in the upper most, right panel is dispersing quickly, because around its average momentum the dispersion relation is strongly curved. Because of the periodicity of our (small) grid, this leads to the interference pattern...

Figure 5: Classical MDS embeddings with compact B-matrix spectra: The top left panel shows the embedding of the spatial Fermionic degrees-of-freedom emerging from a Wigner Hamiltonian in a two-dimensional cartesian space via MDS. The top right panel shows the corresponding embedding for a Pauli field Hamiltonian. We overlay graph edges between embedded Fermionic degrees-of-freedom, with edge color indicating hopping term strength. Embedding coordinates MDS 1 and MDS 2 emerge as the dominant Cartesian dimensions of the embedding, as is implied by the bottom panels. These panels show the ordered spectrum of the B matrix for Wigner (left) and Pauli field reconstruction (right). In classical...
